content.problemStatement:
Polymorphism is one of the fundamental concepts of object-oriented programming (OOP). It allows us to use the same name for different entities, but with different meanings depending on the context. 

In C++, there are two types of polymorphism:


- **Compile-time Polymorphism** - This is also known as early binding or static binding. It occurs when the decision about which function or operation to call is made by the compiler at compile time based on the function's name, arguments, and their types. The two types of Compile-time Polymorphism are -
  - **Function Overloading:** This is a form of polymorphism where you can define multiple functions in a class with the same name but different parameter lists. The appropriate function to call is determined at compile time based on the number or types of arguments passed to it.
  - **Operator Overloading:** C++ allows you to overload operators for your own classes, enabling you to define custom behavior for operators like +, -, *, etc.
- **Runtime Polymorphism** - It occurs when we have overridden methods. Overridden methods are methods that have the same name and parameter list in a base class and in a derived class. The compiler cannot decide which method to call at compile time, because the type of the object that invokes the method is not known at compile time. The method is called at runtime, and the appropriate method is chosen based on the type of the object.

In this module, we will cover Compile-time Polymorphism. We will also cover Runtime Polymorphism in further modules.
constraints:
- $1 \leq T \leq 100$
- $1 \leq N \leq 100$
- $1 \leq M \leq N\cdot(N-1)/2$
- $1 \leq u_i, v_i \leq N$
- $u_i \neq v_i$ for each $1 \leq i \leq M$.
- The sum of $N$ over all test cases won't exceed $100$.
content.inputFormat:
- The first line of input will contain a single integer $T$, denoting the number of test cases.
- Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.
    - The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers $N$ and $M$ — the number of vertices and edges of the graph, respectively.
    - The next $M$ lines describe the edges. The $i$-th of these $M$ lines contains two space-separated integers $u_i$ and $v_i$, denoting an edge between $u_i$ and $v_i$.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output on a new line the number of good subgraphs of $G$, modulo $10^9 + 7$.
